DANGER_READ_THIS_FIRST.banner: EVERY NUMBER IN THIS FILE IS FABRICATED. IT DESCRIBES NO REAL POND, NO REAL FISH, AND NO REAL SORTIE.
DANGER_READ_THIS_FIRST.purpose: This data exists for ONE reason: so the webpage developer has correctly-shaped records to build and test the dashboard and twin against, before real data exists. It shows the SHAPE of the data, not any fact about the world.
DANGER_READ_THIS_FIRST.the_absolute_rule: This data must NEVER appear in a thesis results chapter, a pitch as evidence, or any context implying it is real. The webpage MUST render the illustrative_synthetic badge (see brand-identity.json) prominently on anything derived from it. If this data is ever mistaken for real, the integrity of the entire repository is compromised.
DANGER_READ_THIS_FIRST.how_it_was_made: Hand-constructed to be physically plausible — a realistic diel DO curve, a realistic thermocline, a realistic post-front DO crash — so the charts look believable while being entirely invented. That plausibility is exactly what makes it dangerous if mislabelled.

synthetic_site.label: Synthetic Demo Lake (NOT REAL)
synthetic_site.note: A fictional lake cage site loosely modelled on the described dynamics of Philippine lake cage culture. Any resemblance to a real site is coincidental and unintended.

synthetic_diel_do_profile.description: A fabricated but physically plausible 24-hour DO series at 0.5 m depth, showing the diel cycle: afternoon supersaturation, pre-dawn minimum. Illustrates why sampling time matters.
synthetic_diel_do_profile.the_teaching_point: A manual sample at 09:00 reads 6.4 mg/L (fine). The 05:00 minimum is 2.8 mg/L (acute stress). Same day, same pond. This is the diel sampling bias, illustrated with invented-but-plausible numbers.

annotations.label: Pre-dawn minimum — 2.8 mg/L — nobody is measuring
Typical manual sample time — 6.4 mg/L — looks safe
Supersaturation 11.6 mg/L — large algal crop, big demand tonight

synthetic_depth_profile.description: A fabricated single-station vertical profile showing thermal stratification and hypolimnetic hypoxia — the loaded-gun condition.
synthetic_depth_profile.the_teaching_point: The thermocline sits at ~3-4 m (temp drops from 28.1 to 25.3). Below it: anoxic (0.2-0.9 mg/L), negative ORP (sulfide-generating), acidifying. The surface looks survivable at 3.1 mg/L. A cold rain that mixes this column delivers the anoxic, sulfide-laden bottom water to the fish. This is the mechanism from the Laguna de Bay literature, illustrated with invented numbers.

derived_illustrative.note: computed from the fabricated profile above; still synthetic

synthetic_multichannel_snapshot.description: A fabricated single-instant surface snapshot showing the full channel set, for testing dashboard tile layouts.
synthetic_multichannel_snapshot.note: Every value invented. Included so the dashboard developer can lay out the tile grid, wire up the alert-colour logic, and test the provenance badge — NOT so anyone learns anything about water.

instructions: Use these arrays to build and test charts.
Render the illustrative_synthetic badge on EVERYTHING derived from this file. It should be impossible for a viewer to mistake this for real data.

When real telemetry arrives (repository v2.0.0), delete this file and remove the synthetic badges in the SAME change. A stale synthetic badge on real data is nearly as damaging as real-looking synthetic data.
The diel and depth arrays are deliberately physically plausible so your charts look right. That is a feature for development and a hazard for honesty — hence the badges.
